nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater streams and seasonal pools in northern Madagascar (including Nosy Bé) and Mayotte; tubers tend to persist through dry-season periods.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs around the bulb)
fine gravel or sand with bulb placed on top, not buried
open background area with vertical clearance for tall, broad leaves
clean, well-filtered water with moderate flow

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as silver dollars or buenos aires tetras

summary: Madagascar endemic bulb plant prized for its strikingly quilted, deeply textured dark green leaves. Generally considered a background centerpiece for medium-to-large tanks.
careNotes: Aponogeton boivinianus is a Madagascar-endemic bulb plant (also occurring on Mayotte) noted for its dramatic, deeply quilted dark green leaves that have been reported to reach roughly 40-60 cm in cultivation (with some sources documenting up to 80 cm under ideal conditions). It is generally advised as a background or solitary focal specimen in tanks of about 150 L or larger, with enough vertical clearance for the broad, textured foliage. The bulb should be placed on top of the substrate or only half-buried — fully burying it tends to cause rot. Temperatures around 22-26 °C, pH roughly 6.5-7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (about 3-15 dGH) are commonly recommended; it tends to do best with cooler, very clean water than its tropical relatives. Medium to high light combined with nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs) gives the best growth; CO2 injection is not required but is often reported to improve leaf quality and growth rate. It is commonly considered more demanding than A. crispus but easier than A. madagascariensis, owing to its preference for clean water and its dormancy behaviour. IMPORTANT: this species has a natural dormancy cycle — after roughly 7-8 months of growth (often following flowering), the leaves typically die back and the bulb rests for several weeks to a few months. Do NOT discard the bulb when this happens; leave it in the substrate (or store it in cool, damp conditions) and new leaves typically resprout. Note: some retailers mislabel A. boivinianus as 'Madagascar Lace Plant', which is properly A. madagascariensis (a different, much more demanding species with truly perforated lattice-like leaves).
breedingNotes: Generally propagated from seed produced on emergent flower spikes (the species can self-pollinate when allowed to flower above the water surface). Mature plants occasionally form daughter tubers that can be separated; tuber/bulb division is the most reliable propagation method in aquaria. Flowering and seed-set are reported to be uncommon under typical fully submerged conditions.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Originally-designated Buce Plant primary URL returned 404; promoted Aquarium Co-Op (secondary) to primary. Confirmed background placement, fast growth, and 7-8 month grow / 1-2 month dormancy cycle. Note: Co-Op's product page calls it 'Madagascar Lace Plant', which is incorrect (that name properly belongs to A. madagascariensis).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Aponogetonaceae), order (Alismatales), Madagascar endemism, ~60 cm height, quilted dark green leaves, prefers cooler clean water, seed propagation.

additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (N. Madagascar / Nosy Bé / Mayotte), height 30-80 cm, optimal temperature 22-26 °C, pH 6-8, medium-to-high light, fast growth, propagation by seeds and daughter plants, 'easy' difficulty rating.
